THE HISTORIAN OF THE PRESBYTERIAN   CHORCH IN IRELAND The   announcement that the Theological  Daculty of his Ohurch hes   conferred the degree of Doctor in  Divinity on the Rev . William Thomas Latimer will be received with great  satisfaction by all Fho know  of the man aud his work. Dr . Latimer is a native of Co Tyrone, in #hich his life has been   spent. His father, the Rev . John Latimer , who died in 1843, shortly after the birth of his only   child, was minister of Firgt Ballynahatty Presbyterian   Church, near Omagh. His   mother was a Dudgeon of Mullaghmore, whose family had held property there for  considerably overr two hundred years. Her   father, William Dudgeon, was robbed, in 1791, by Tom Eccles, for which crime that famous outlaw was afterwards   hanged, A bad attack of pneumonia at the age of  threo produced 2 delicacy in Dr . Latimer' 8 constitution, against  which he has had to fight all his life. It inter - fered Fith his attendance at school, but did not prevent   him from reading   everything that camo HILS way . Before he was ten he bad read book in his father' s library -theology, poetry, history, every and encyclopaedia in fourteen volumes [ With ittle especially an assistance from outside he prepared for matriculation, and entered Queen' $ College, Belfast, passed creditably through the various obtaining prizes, and graduated B.A in 1870 . After clagses, tho at the Presbyterian College, Belfast, he usual cour8e Jicensed to preach, and almost immediately To- wag duly ceived & call to be minister of Eglish, noar Dunganuon, and ordained on October 7th, 1872. Here ho mhich he accepted, Wab hag lived quiet life 'of the country parson, and the strenuous   lifo the of the laboriou:, paingtaking historian. Living neal Dungannon   af and close by tho   battlefield  of Benburb, it i8 not tbe Volunteerg " that Dr . Latimer' 9 attontion was turned at an to be wondered at carly period in his ministerial lifo to higtorical  gbudics. His Histary 1) i8 a histary  of 2 peoplo 08 well {5 of 0 cf tho Irigh  Presbyteriang It won high prai8o from no le8s an authority religicug denomination.

than W . E. H. Locky. In all   his writings he has shown n most scrupulous regard for accuracy of statement and 3 never-failing inde pendonce of thought. Ho has never written to please a public, but has freely criticised all   parties in Church   and State. It ha8 been written of bin Mr . Latimer has gathered an immense library. In   fact, the Manso is   largely   looked on by him a8 2 good place to store books . He is 0 wide reader,  always well  versed in the latest developmeuts of modern thought, and literally soaked in the   achieve - ments   of the past. Ho is 1 brilliant conversationalist, and an bour passed in his company is time well spent. His smiles and his thunder are both  inspiring: He is 2 young man if judged by his ideals, and a very old man if  judged by his knowledge. He is 2 very austere man if judged by his invcctives, and 2 very kind man if judged by bis actions In 1910 the Queen' s University of Belfast conferred on him the degree of M.A,, his published work being accepted in lieu of 2 thesis and now comes this   crorning honour from the Ohurch he has served B0 well. Long may he live to enjcy it ! BIBLIOGRAPHY . Lecturo on the Doctrines of the Plymouth Brethren, 1882 7th edition, 16th thousand, 1908 History of the Life and Times of the Rev Henry Cooke, D.D, LL D. PRINTING IN LOUGHREA, 1825-1882. In Vol. II,, p. 151, I gave & list  of the  earliest works printed in this Galway town' from 1766 to 1825, which had been hitherto unknown to  bibliographers. John Power gave 1834, and Arrchdeacon Cotton 1838, as  the earliest dates known to them . The art of printing ap parently died out with Richardson about 1825, to bo resuscitated by the Kelly family as pointed out by Mr. R. J. Kelly, K.O., at Vol. I,, p. 181 . This would account for the great gap between 1825 and 1838, for, aS usual, Power gives no titles, and I have not come aoross any item bearing date 1834 . With Thomas Kelly' 8 death the art again died out. 1838 . On the Necessity of reforming the Religious orders in Ireland: 8 (Vide Cotton's 9) (Thomas   Kelly.) 8vo , Pp. Typo graphical Gazetteer, second   serie8, P. 126.) NOTE_Printed privately. (1840.) To the Hon _ The  Knights, Oitizens and  Burgesses. in Par- liament   assembled. The Humble Petition  of the Co. of Cialway. (U.L,C. IB., Hib. 5, 840, 5,) 1846. (Thomas Kelly, Main Btreet.) (Vide Slater' 9 Directory ) 1857 , January, ctc. Tho Loughrea Journal. (Vido nexf item.) 1858. Journal. A Monthly   Miscellany af The Loughrea  Illustrated Art, Scienco and Local Affiairg . Vol. II,, No8. Literature, 17 and 18 (Mar and Juno, 1858) Price %d. AN  IRISH   BLUE STOCKING. 'Ihe Blue Stocking Movement reached its heyday between 1756 and 1788, and during those years social life waS brought to a perfeo tion in   Dngland that it had never attained before, and has never whose (( Bas Bleu W attained since. Haunah More, poem is 9 microcosm of the movement, tells uS The sole purpose of these assemblies wa8 conversation, and   they Ferre  different in n0 respect from other  parties, save that the company did not play at cards." The hostesses who inaugurated and presided over the Bas Bleu assemblies were an Englishwoman, a Welshwoman; and an Irisb- woman, cach of them  respectively strongly endowed with the con- ventional national characteristics_Mrs. Montagu, Mrs. Thrale and Mrs. Vesey . Frrom our point of view the most interesting and most elusive of the group ig Mrg Vesey , the Irighwoman Though Mrs.  Montagu i8 generally   accepted Queen of the Blues,' 0) yet to Mrs . Vesey Hannah More assigng first place , and dedicates to her the poem mentioned; Mrg.   Veser has left nothing behind but a few short and ecattered   letter8, no fragment of   autobiography, no scrap of self- revealation. Sho lives for u8 simply through the records of her English friendg ; wO see her   only   through English eye8 . Out of numeroug yellow worm-caten old volumes she emerges delicately ) alive. She wag the daughtcr of Sir Thomas Vesey Bishop of Ossory, and wB; twice married, in both case8 to memberg of the Irish Parlia- ment , her gecond  hueband   being albo Accountant Gencral for Ireland. To mo she gcems to havo btrayed out of another century and another ephere, one who held her placo in those crowded assomblies by  sheer magic of  pergonality. Oeltic to her finger tips, congistcnt in hcr

TE IRISH   BOOK LOVER.
177
inconsistencies,  viewing the warld as  an uneal and   faery   place;,  big with Faguo possibilities, and   sbrong   emotions, she seem8 a creature of a different   element, and her stolid  English  friends,  awakened   by contact with her into wnusual perception, bestowed upon her  tha title of The   Sylph . Dr Johnsou'8 friends  awoke in him a strong admir- ation  for Ireland , her great traditions ad her   language. So I may claim that Mrs. Vesey   exerted an almost  equal   influence upon herr women friends inspiring them with understanding of Irish character and  Irish  ideals, imbuing them with love and sympathy for Ireland. The mention of her in letters and memoirs is  always   accompanied by little outbursts of   feeling: Her   qualities of heart Fere of ttie olost   winning sort, kindness, sympathy and absolute   self-forgetful- DSS , generosity, hospitality . Horace   Walpole bay8 of her , What Englishh heart ever excelled hers ? I should almost have said equalled. 2) Elizabeth Carter , the tranglator  of  Epiotetus, Frote to her , Your imagination is a5  lively and picturesque now a8 it could hare been at  eighteen_ J) Mrs. Vesey Fas 0 dreamer, with all the restlessness, all the   impracticability of those who dream dreams She was haunted with tho desire of a perfection she could never attain . But instead of pursuing her   ideal   through lonely  meditation Or fery adventure, we find her geeking for it in the crowded festivities of London Strange and   pathetic  quest, thhe   search for the   country of the young, the Land of Heart's Desire in the comfortable common- place of the  eighteenth  cenbury. To her assemblies the most dis tinguished men and women of the day iocked  gladly, Garriok , John- eon   and Burke amongst them_ This is Hannah More's description of them Here sober duchesses aro seen, Chaste wits and critics void of  spleen ; Physicians   fraught   with real   science, And Whigs   and Tories  in aliance. Poots   fulilling Chrisbian duties, Just  lawvyens,  reasonable   bbeauties ; Bishops   who preacll, and peers   #ho pay, who seldom   play. " And   coumitosse8 I Vesey'g   leadning: She did have no tangiblo proofis to shhow of  Mir8. not writo books, and cf hcr lebtcts,   which a contemiporary g8 "were

178
remarkably   beautiful; but ` few remain. The close of her life Wa8 clouded in gloom . Her husband died in 1785, Icaving, Ie aro told, (4 his  accounts in both worlds   unsettled." Her faculties   began to fail, but  she was  cared for by Mrs.  Handcock, the sister"   of her first husband. She lingered on into 1791. Even in her darkest years her friends continued faithdul,  though she no longer knew them_ Tha only one amongst them whose attention gave her the smallest pleasure, was  Horace Walpole, whom To are apt to think of as only a fashionable dilettanti. But we caw nOw add  another leaf to his chaplet of fame for his unceasing kindness to Mrs . Vesey _ 70( Tho Irish Blue Stocking  )) ETHDL ROLT WEELIR.
MAUREEN .
You   remember how he came in the  glory of his youth, With his beauty and his truth and his , winning wayg. You remember how he went at the summons England sent, And the weary, dreary waiting of those autumn days.
Ah, his sadness of farewell, when you waved your last adieu Waved your pretty hand to him standing o the stair  Gazing from your dreamy eyes, your eyes of Irish blue With a rose upon your bosom and a blossom in your hair.
Spring will burst in bud again, and summer days come back to u8 ; Mons, like a wistful memory, fado when Time'$ grey wings have fown. Has the love you might have given found its way at last to heaven, From the  little lonely  chapel on the road to Bandon town ?
Oh, blue eye8 dull with weeping for your English  lover, sleeping, And thoge fowera of dear remembrance that you woro whilst kneeling there _ Death had eded all forgiving, but becauso ho loved them, living, roso wa8 in your bobom  a  blossom in your   hair [ RANDAL McDONNELL:

IRISH NOVELISTS. (Vol:. VI: , p: 169.) Father Brown is to be congratulated on the success  of hi8 6 Guide to Irish Fiction." The Pig-Driving Peelers; )) by Kickham , is not 2 story, but a mare episode from For the Old Land;" extracted by Mr. Yeats, and probably othhers, for anthological purposes  if 80 hideous a phrase may be permitted. It i8 given in the selections from Irish novelists edited by Mr . Yeats for   the Knickerbocker ' Series published by Messrs. Putnam and Sons. and Tho Orangeman, wcr0 reprinted in The Shamrock W) boy in the early nineties of last cenbury by Edmund Leamy at my-suggestion, Who were the Forgus MacDire and (( MacErin 0"Tara, who pub- lished novels about the middle of last century ? I am specially inter- ested in Selina Bunbury, author of Tales   of My Country, 1833, (6 The Abbey of Innismoyle, 1) 1828, Darly Recollections, 9) and would be glad to have any information about her . I quote 2 letter of hers to Carleton in my biography of the latter. She was 2 voluminous writer. Allibone (Vol. I.) credits her also with Coombe Abbey, 1843; Evelyn, 2 vols-, 1849 ; Evenings in the Pyrenees, 2 vols.  1848 ; Rides } in the Pyrenees, 2 vols , 1844; Star of the Court, 1845 ; Life in Sw eden, 2 vols. ; Russia  aftcr the War, 2 vols. , 1857 and nine other works down to 1870 in his supploment. JOTTINGS ON   JOURNALS The Journal RS.AI. has for  frontispiece a portrait of the late James   Mills, I.S.Ow, for some time its   editor, follored by 2 sym- pathetic   appreciation of his   life work over the well known initials B.F B Mr . P. E: Bagenal, who used to be a prominent figure iu Irish literature and  politics, makes a welcome re-appearance, with an able sketch, the result of deep research, of the once powerful Marshal, Sir  Nicholas   Bagenal. Dr. Berry deals with the Ancient Deeds  of the Parish of St. Werburgh, 1' whilst Messrs. Armstrong and Westropp; respectively, conbribute two well-illustrated and very interesting articles on A Processional   Oross, and other articles discovered neal Oldbridge in  1899, and The Prehistoric Remains in Burren;" Co Clare. The Editor of the Waterford   Archeological Journal, Rev . P. Power , continues in its pages his   scholaaly article on The Subsoil of Jrish History." Mr . M, Butler concludes his   article on Crotty the Robber," and continues his  correspondence on New Geneva. Dr . Grattan Flood; Mi . Seamus   0'Casaide, and otlerg contribute notes, literary and  historical, of more bhan loca] interest. Tho waT and its concomitant of martial music, looms largo in tho pages of tho "Journal of tho Irish Tolk  Song  Sociely, 91 whose anuual volume is before u8. Amongst   tlle conbributors ar0 Dr . Gratban Flood, who, writing on Irigh Marcheg ' olaim: that Tho Cirl I left bohind mo, long known in England undor the title of "Brighton Oamp;' is purely Irish, having bcen printed in Dublin 49 long ago as 1794, and known

traditionally much  earlier . The editor, Mrs. Milligan Tox, pay8 2 just tribute to the war" poetry written by Mr. Perceval Graves, and dealg rather  drastioally with the Feis Coel Collection of Airg in a lengthy revien She asks, iu referenco to Sir Fredk. Bridge's mention of Ninety-Five, J) does  he mean 98 ? 7] No, he doesn't He mean8 the fine marching tune of tho old 95th Regiment, to which the men used to sing Im ninety-five, 1' m ninety-five, and to keep single I contrive. 1) Some balf-dozen folk airs collected by Miss Colthurst around  Blarney and  Ballyvourney and phonographed by Mrs. Tox, are amongst the choicest features of the journal.

EDITORS   GOSSIP In Ohambens' & Journal " for May Mr . A G, Bradley, in the courSe of an interesting article on Judge Lynch, )) tells u5 that ( Charles Iynch, 8 Galway   settler in  Virginia, whose name is now eynonymous with a barbarous contempt for criminal law , wa8 no wild, bloodthirsty   Irishman, a8 even Americang often  imagine, but a sim- ple, God-fearing Quaker and  worthy . gentleman, 2 deservedly respect- ed chairman of magistrates " Tn the British Roview 9) Katharino  Tynan has 3 poem   entitled 66 Unbouseled, Unanointed, Unancled, and another one The   Open Road" in the "Cornhill; to   which also the writer  who elects to bo known a8 John Haslette, 9) contributes an angling sketch, Tho Vet eran;" Last, but not   least, Mr . T. W . Rolleston   disousse8 German Literature in the opening articlo of thc Quarterly Reviow," I sent the original leaveg af Neill'g New Tostament, only 0 portion cf which appeared in tho faogimilo prosented  with last numbor; to the Linen Hall  Librory, Belfast, a9 the most fitting permanent resting

TAW IRISH BOOR  LOVIR.
183
place for such 4 rarity. For the past generation that library hag been the receptacle for all such items of local initerest, and now , by dona- tion ,  legacy , or loan, it contains the greatest number of Belfast printed books to bo found   anynhere. As is   only natural and in tbe ftoess of things, I might also sy in this connection that had it' not been for the liberality of its Board of Governors, Mr_ John Andergon could never have produced his valuable   catalogues of Darly Belfast Printed Books. Just a word of welcome and good wishes for a dourishing career to the new literary and artistic quarterly, The Gypsy,' which made its bow to 2 discriminating public on 17th May. More   especially as the associate editor; Mr. Louis J. McQuilland is 2 Belfast man and formerly private secretary to Mr. John Redmond . Many of my read- ers in that shipbuilding city will remember the Kit-Kat Club, COm - posed of (( free lances of palette and pen,'' the  only really Bohemian club that ever existed there, and of which Mr. McQuilland was one of the founders. Its chief claim to remembrance is that some half- dozen of its members Were responsible for a charming volume of verse Sung by Six " (Belfast, 1896), in which Mr. McQuilland hit off the ambitions and foibles of its habitues in verses that live in our memory yet. Each  rbymster Fas one  with the muses, And each dauber was king of his  art; Should reviews andreviewers abuse 48, We ' smiled while TB suffered the smart. We were graced with great masters of   fiction, Whom with the immortals we classed, Though the publio gave flat  contradiction To tlhe fat our judgment had passed " To those of my readers, and I know there are many , who collect autographs, it will be of interest to learn that Messrs:  Maggs, of the Gtrand , have just  isgued 0 new illugtrated   catalogue,  turned   out in the highest gtyle of arb. Amongst many  item8 I note @ few of par - ticular Irish interest. for Duffy, whoso fine, truthful  intellect aud ardent human   character were always  recognizable to mo in the worst tumult of Irish con- fusions. NOTICES O1 NEW BOOKS. Tne LIFE OF PnILIP  SKELTON . By Samuel  Burdy , A.B. With an introduction by Normau Moore. (Oxford Clarendon Press) . 5s. This, the fourth edition of this most interesting biography , and an exact reprint of the original_published in Dublin in 1792 , is ably and sympathetically edited by that scholarly   physician Dr. Norman  Moore, #ho wrote in the DN.B. the first biography of Burdy ever  published, and like him has personally visited many of the scenes associated Fith the life work of Skelton rith happy results Skelton wag boru of pOor   parents at Derriaghy, which, by the way, is in Co . Antrim, tbough on the borders of Down, aud educated at Trinity. He visited London 0n several occasiona to publish his books, and made his mark there aS 2 preacher Preferment came to him late, the living of Pettigo, and rhen there he wrote one of the earliest publshed accouuts of Lough Derg and its  pilgrimage, and has left behind hm in ould Donegal a name still remembered for boundless charity, which impelled him once in time of  famine to  scll his   library__what a wrench for 2 scholar ~to buy meal for his starving parishioners of every creed. As a curate passing rich on forty pounds 2 year, he was known to give eway one half of it and live o the other These traits, combined wilh hig powers a8 a preacher , his herculean strength, his homely ways, his talent as a conversationalist, and his many  publications_his works in five volumes Fere published by  subscription in 1770 for the benefit of the Magdalen Asylum, Dublin--render his memory fragrant. What 2 strangely assorted pair are the subject of this life and its author, the one  big and burly, full of fight ; the other mild, humble and a self- conscious hero  worshipper. Dr . Mooro has done good service by bis introductory life of Burdy, beside whom Skelton stands out a grandily commanding fgure. It may be added that the book is furnished   with an excellent index which Wa8 wanting in tho previous editions. IRIBI  SKTTCHES . By F. I. Woodwright. (Drane.) ls. Tho his- toric scenes and bcauty spots   sketched herein havo often been des- oribed, and by groater writers ; yet Miss Woodwright fnds   something fresh and interesting to gay about thom There is nothing hackneyed in hor pagos, and ono accompanieg her in funcy to ronOW acquaint- with Tara, Blarnoy , Lissoy and other  places of  interest, with ances much pleasure. Theso   sho hag al8o deftly   delinoated with   hea"   pencil and thus added & charm to her frst _bub not, WO anticipate, her last _ book.

TIE Boor OF' TITS AND TIAT. By Robert Lynd. (Mills and Boon.) 4s . Gd . One wonders if it be at all  possible to carry  out 1 dictum of the author   and  let 0 review be 2 portrait of a book . Let 48 try The volume is reat and light in handling, tastefully turned out, devoid of tinsel, with plain paper label, and at once attracts atten - tion in a row of others. Its contents arc somo eight and twenty essays that have appeared from time to timo in the New States- man, dealing with such diverse subjects, amongst others, a8 ( G. B S. and the Bishops,' 2) Tango Teas; )) The Right to Murder;' Chistmas; J) Demagogues, 2) Spring Fashions, )) Black Oats,' 1} and The Decline and Fall of Hell,"' which latter would make some of the author'g old friends  turn in their graves. That they are clever , graceful and witty goes   without  saying: They ar0 by no means  the vapid  outsprings of the  ordinary journalist, for in each and all the author has something fresh to say , some new point of view , some original idea to give utterance to. Have Te succeeded ? No ; we  feel Wo are but indifferent portraitists   and have failed   entirely to convey any idea of the soul of the book. Go, therefore, reader, and buy the volume for yourself: Read, re-read, and treasure it. DER KAISER Von PoTSDAM . By B. B. Burton. (Hodges and Figgis.) If this bo a first attempt, a8 we take it to Je, it displays some knowledgo of the craft. It invites recognition and deserves encouragement. STRAIGHT AND CRoOKED . By James F. Cousins .
